nativeRange.habitat: Shallow margins of ponds, lakes, marshes, and slow-moving streams; tolerates brackish coastal wetlands at the low end of its salinity range

avoidWith: vigorously digging fish (large loaches, geophagine cichlids)

summary: A classic foreground carpeting plant with fine grass-like blades that spreads aggressively via substrate runners to form a lush lawn. Demanding by hobby standards: high light and CO2 are needed for a tight, low carpet; under marginal conditions it grows tall and lax.
careNotes: Eleocharis parvula is the standard 'Dwarf Hairgrass' of the aquarium trade and one of the most popular foreground carpet plants. Naming note: E. parvula and the larger E. acicularis are routinely conflated in the trade and even sold interchangeably; most plants labeled 'Dwarf Hairgrass' in retail are E. parvula (max ~10–12 cm), while E. acicularis tends slightly taller (~10–20 cm) — the names below the species rank ('Mini', 'Belem', etc.) are cultivar selections under E. parvula or close relatives. Lighting is the single most important parameter: under medium-to-high light (PAR ~50+ at substrate) the plant carpets low and tight; under low light it runs vertical and sparse, often failing as a carpet altogether. CO2 is technically optional but in practice strongly recommended for the dense carpet look most aquascapers want — without CO2 the plant typically survives but will not form a true tight lawn. It is a moderate root feeder: a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or inert substrate with root tabs gives substantially better results than plain gravel. Temperature 22–28 °C is the comfortable hobby range, with reported tolerance from ~18–28 °C. Propagation is by substrate runners that spread laterally from the parent rosette; trimming the carpet short after establishment encourages denser horizontal growth. New plantings should be separated into small clumps (3–5 blades each) and planted on a grid roughly 2–3 cm apart for fastest fill-in. Algae on the blades is a common early problem; running CO2 and lean nitrate/phosphate dosing during establishment helps.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only in aquarium settings: spreads by horizontal underground runners (rhizomatous stolons) that emerge as new rosettes a few centimeters from the parent. Established carpets can be lifted, divided into small clumps, and replanted to expand coverage or restart a tank. Flowering and seed production are possible under emersed conditions but essentially never occur in submerged aquarium use.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Confirmed temperature range 72–82 °F (22–28 °C), medium-to-high lighting (stronger light keeps carpet low and dense), CO2 strongly responsive but not strictly required, moderate growth rate accelerating with CO2/light/substrate, foreground placement, runner propagation through substrate, nutrient-rich substrate preferred (Fluval Stratum / root tabs), moderate care level — easy to keep alive, harder to achieve a true tight carpet.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check: medium-to-high lighting required, runner-based propagation forms a lush carpet, moderately easy care level. Page did not specify CO2/temp/pH/hardness numerics; used Modern Aquarium for those.
